Executive Summary

Executive Summary

This is a high-pressure, VMC day over the West — the active weather system is locked in the Pacific Northwest and will have cleared well before your Monday evening departure. Primary Risk: terrain-driven wind and possible localized mountain wave turbulence over the Cascades and Sierra Nevada at 12,000 ft. The broader pattern by March 30 shows ridging rebuilding over the western CONUS after the current Pacific system sweeps through, giving you a clean VMC flight from Bend to San Carlos. This is a GO with solid confidence, contingent on winds aloft confirmation closer to departure. Pay attention to mountain wave potential — it won't keep you on the ground, but it could make for a rough ride at cruise altitude.

Operational Briefing

The Day Before — What to Watch

Sunday evening, March 29, pull up the latest:

  • Winds aloft forecast (FB winds) at 12,000 ft for stations along your route — BKE, RDM, OAK, SFO. You're looking for whether the westerly flow has backed off behind the departing system. If winds aloft are still honking at 50+ knots out of the west over the Cascades or Sierra, expect moderate to rough turbulence at cruise.
  • G-AIRMETs for turbulence and mountain obscuration over the OR/CA Cascades and the Sierra Nevada. If the mountain wave AIRMET is active at 12,000 ft on your route, plan to cruise lower (9,000–10,000 ft) or higher (above wave tops) if your aircraft and oxygen support it.
  • Updated surface prog for Mon 18–00Z — confirm the high pressure is positioned where expected. If a secondary trough or low develops off the Pacific Coast faster than forecast, coastal marine layer at KSQL could lower ceilings on arrival.
  • KSQL coastal marine layer — Check METARs at KSQL, KHAF, and KSFO Sunday night. If marine stratus is sitting at 500–1,000 ft AGL over the Bay Area coast, that could complicate your VFR arrival. This is the one wildcard to watch.

If Sunday night shows the ridge firmly in place and winds aloft are moderate (under 40 kts at 12,000), you're good to go as planned.

Leaving KBDN — Departure at 2026-03-30 21:30Z

No TAF coverage yet for KBDN at departure time, so this is chart-based. By March 30 at 21:30Z (2:30 PM local Pacific), the pattern over central Oregon supports clear to few clouds and good VFR visibility. The Pacific system that's the focus of the current active charts will have exited the region by Saturday at the latest — the Day 5 and Day 6 extended progs show ridging rebuilding over the Pacific Northwest and interior West by Sunday–Monday.

Departure conditions at Bend/Bend Muni should be:

  • Ceilings: Few to clear — no IMC signal at KBDN at departure time
  • Visibility: Unrestricted — no precipitation or fog expected
  • Surface winds: Terrain-modified — Bend sits in a valley east of the Cascades. Post-frontal ridge flow typically produces light westerly to northwesterly surface winds. Expect 8–15 kts, possibly gusting to 20 kts if the westerly gradient is still tightening. Confirm with day-of METARs.
  • Climb-out: Expect VMC through your climb. No layer to punch through. The Cascades to your west are the only moisture source, and by departure time the active system is long gone.

Getting out of KBDN is a non-event for this day. Your concern starts after wheels-up — terrain and winds en route.

The Ride at 12,000 ft

Icing

No icing charts were provided in this briefing package, and no significant QPF is shown along the KBDN–KSQL route on March 30. The Day 6 and Day 7 extended progs show no precipitation signals and no frontal boundaries along or near your route at departure time.

No widespread moisture indicated along the route at flight time. Without a cloud/moisture field overlapping the route and altitude band, there is no meaningful icing signal to report. Freezing level at 12,000 ft in late March over central Oregon and northern California is likely near or above cruise altitude depending on the airmass behind the ridge — but without supporting icing chart data, this is LOW CONFIDENCE detail. Treat it as follows:

AltitudeIcing RiskNotes
12,000 ftCONDITIONAL / LOWNo moisture signal on route; clear air expected
8,000–10,000 ftCONDITIONAL / LOWPossible localized cloud on Cascades western slopes — not on your route
Below 8,000 ftMinimalPost-frontal, drying airmass east of Cascades

No SLD signal present. SLD is not a factor for this flight based on available data.

Turbulence and Ride Quality

This is the main operational concern. Your route crosses:

  • The Oregon Cascades — departing KBDN westbound, you'll be descending the lee side or skirting south toward the Siskiyous
  • The Klamath/Siskiyou terrain complex — irregular high terrain between southern Oregon and northern California
  • The northern Sierra Nevada — depending on routing, terrain influence continues into central California

At 12,000 ft MSL with a westerly flow pattern behind a departing Pacific system, mountain wave turbulence is a CONDITIONAL hazard — present if the post-frontal jet remains strong over the terrain. The Day 6/7 charts suggest a rebuilding high, which typically means moderating winds, but the transition period between the departing system and the ridge can produce residual wave activity.

No winds aloft data was provided for this briefing package — this is a critical gap. On the day before, decode the FB winds for BKE (Burns, OR), RDM (Redmond, OR), OAK (Oakland, CA), and SFO at 12,000 ft. That number drives your turbulence exposure directly.

  • Winds at 12,000 ft under 30 kts: smooth to light chop, no wave concern
  • Winds at 12,000 ft 30–45 kts: moderate chop possible over terrain, brief and manageable
  • Winds at 12,000 ft over 45 kts: mountain wave turbulence POSSIBLE — consider altitude adjustment or timing delay

Other Hazards

HazardRiskLegWhat to Expect
Mountain wave / rotor turbulenceCONDITIONALKBDN to mid-CADepends on winds aloft — check day-before FB winds
Coastal marine stratus at KSQLLOCALIZED / CONDITIONALArrivalLate afternoon — marine layer possible but not dominant in high-pressure pattern
ConvectionMinimal / not a factorEntire routeNo convective signals on any chart for this date/route

Getting Into KSQL

No TAF coverage yet for KSQL at arrival time (~23:59Z / 4 PM local Pacific). Chart-based assessment:

By late afternoon Monday, the Bay Area is under high pressure with the Pacific system long gone. This is a VMC arrival in most scenarios. The primary wildcard at San Carlos/San Carlos is the coastal marine layer — late afternoon is actually the time when marine stratus is least likely to be a problem (it typically burns off mid-morning and doesn't return until evening/night). Your 23:59Z arrival (4 PM local) is in the sweet spot — after the morning burn-off and before the evening marine push.

That said, in a post-frontal onshore flow pattern, the marine layer can be persistent and deeper than usual. Check KSQL, KHAF, and KSFO METARs the morning of to confirm the layer has cleared.

Alternates: KSJC (San Jose Mineta) or KPAO (Palo Alto) are good alternates if KSQL is socked in with marine stratus. Both are slightly inland and typically have better ceilings than coastal KSQL when the marine layer is active. KMOD (Modesto) further inland is a solid alternate if the entire Bay Area coastal strip is below minimums.

Estimated block time: KBDN to KSQL is roughly 380 nm. At 12,000 ft with headwinds typical of westerly post-frontal flow, plan 2:30–3:00 hrs. Crosswinds at KSQL — Runway 12/30 — with westerly surface flow should be manageable; wind direction at the surface may be SW to W, giving a crosswind component on Runway 30. Confirm on arrival ATIS.

Fly or No

GO — with a standard pre-flight weather check the night before and morning of.

Hard stops (don't go if):

  • Winds aloft at 12,000 ft are still 50+ kts westerly along the Oregon/California Cascades — mountain wave turbulence becomes a real concern, especially for light GA
  • G-AIRMET for severe mountain wave is active along your route at cruise altitude
  • KSQL METAR on departure shows OVC below 500 ft with no improvement trend, and alternates are also below minimums

Watch list:

  • Winds aloft — this is the swing factor. Moderate winds = smooth ride. Strong winds = rough terrain crossing.
  • Marine layer at KSQL — a non-issue if you check it the morning of and confirm burn-off

There is no weather system threatening this flight. The risk is all terrain and wind — both of which are manageable with good pre-flight planning and appropriate altitude selection.

Abort Criteria

  • Winds aloft at 12,000 ft reported or forecast >50 kts along the Cascades/Sierra corridor — delay departure by 3–6 hours or file lower (9,000 ft) to get below the wave layer
  • PIREPs reporting severe turbulence over Oregon/northern California terrain at cruise altitude
  • KSQL below 500 ft OVC with KSJC, KPAO, and KMOD also marginal (no suitable alternate available)
  • Developing coastal low or trough not in the current forecast moving onshore ahead of schedule — would be visible in morning prog updates

If You Go — Do This

  1. Sunday evening: Pull FB winds for BKE, RDM, OAK at 9,000, 12,000, and 18,000 ft. Decode and write down the 12,000 ft component for each. This is your single most important pre-flight data point.
  2. Sunday evening: Check G-AIRMET Sierra and Cascade for mountain turbulence and mountain obscuration. Note whether it's active at or above 12,000 ft.
  3. Monday morning: Check KSQL, KSFO, KHAF METARs at ~14–15Z to confirm the marine layer is clearing on schedule. If OVC 500 ft is still sitting on the coast at 16Z, give it until 19Z and re-check before you commit to departure.
  4. Altitude: If winds aloft at 12,000 ft are 35–45 kts, consider 10,000 ft or 14,000 ft (if oxygen equipped) to find a smoother layer. If smooth at 12,000, stay there — you'll be above most terrain concerns.
  5. Routing: File Victor airways or a direct route that keeps you east of the Cascade crest as long as practical before turning southwest toward the Bay. Crossing the Cascades at a lower elevation notch (rather than straight over high terrain) reduces wave exposure.
  6. Alternates: File KSJC or KPAO as your alternate. Have KMOD in your back pocket if the whole Bay Area coast goes below minimums.
  7. IFR filing: File IFR even if you expect VMC the whole way — the terrain routing, the altitude, and the coastal arrival all benefit from the structure and ATC backup. This isn't about weather — it's about professionalism and options.

What Forecasters Are Saying

  • No AFD was provided for this briefing package. All assessments are chart-based only.
  • The extended progs (Day 6/7) show agreement on high pressure rebuilding over the western CONUS by March 29–30, but confidence at Day 6–7 range is inherently MEDIUM — timing of the ridge axis and the strength of onshore flow are not locked in at this range.
  • The key uncertainty is the transition period between the departing Pacific system (Days 1–3) and the rebuilding high. If the system slows or the ridge is weaker than forecast, residual cloudiness and stronger winds over terrain are possible on March 30.
  • The coastal marine layer behavior at KSQL is a local terrain effect not well-resolved in extended charts — day-of METARs are your best data source for this specific risk.

Confidence

Overall Confidence: MEDIUM

The synoptic picture is clear and consistent — a Pacific system sweeps through, a high rebuilds, and your flight day looks clean. That part is solid. What drops this from HIGH to MEDIUM is:

  • No winds aloft data was available — the single biggest operational variable for this flight is unknown at time of briefing
  • No AFD provided — forecaster nuance on the timing and strength of the post-system ridge is missing
  • Day 6–7 chart confidence is inherently limited — by Sunday evening, re-run this check with updated 36–48 hr progs
  • Coastal marine layer behavior at KSQL is a local day-of question — not resolvable from charts this far out

If you pull the winds aloft Sunday evening and they're moderate, bump this to HIGH confidence. If they're strong with wave AIRMETs active, re-evaluate altitude and timing before committing to the 21:30Z departure.
